items.foco: Most common cause of infectious keratitis
items.title: Most common cause
items.contentHtml:
Epidemiology

  • The most common cause of infectious keratitis in developed countries is infection with herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1).
  • In tropical regions, bacterial keratitis (especially Pseudomonas in contact lens wearers) may be more prevalent, but in temperate climates HSV-1 leads.
  • Herpetic keratitis represents the main cause of unilateral infectious corneal blindness in the world.
  • Latency in the trigeminal ganglion allows recurrent reactivations, which increases the cumulative incidence.

items.title: VHS-1
items.contentHtml:
Virology

  • The herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) is a neurotropic, double-stranded DNA virus that establishes latency in the trigeminal ganglia.
  • Primary infection is usually asymptomatic or manifests as herpetic gingivostomatitis; Keratitis typically occurs by reactivation.
  • The virus replicates in the corneal epithelium, causing direct cytolysis and an inflammatory response.
  • Detection by PCR or viral culture confirms the diagnosis, although clinical presentation with fluorescein staining is usually sufficient.

items.title: Epithelial dendrite
items.contentHtml:
Classic presentation

  • The epithelial dendrite is the pathognomonic lesion of epithelial keratitis due to HSV-1.
  • It is observed as a branching ulcer with terminal thickenings (bulbous buttons) that stain with fluorescein.
  • Clinically: ocular pain, photophobia, lacrimation, foreign body sensation and decreased corneal sensitivity.
  • The differential diagnosis includes varicella-zoster virus keratitis (smaller dendrite without terminal bulbs) and traumatic epithelial defects (without branches).

items.title: Fluorescein staining
items.contentHtml:
Key finding

  • Fluorescein stain: arboriform defect enhancing the epithelial dendrite with thin edges and thickening at the ends.
  • Fluorescein accumulates in the epithelial defect, showing an image similar to a branched tree.
  • Should be examined with a slit lamp and cobalt filter for maximum visualization.
  • Rose Bengal or lissamine green staining can also highlight infected cells, but fluorescein is the standard test.

items.title: Corneal hypoesthesia
items.contentHtml:
Clinical sign

  • Decreased corneal sensitivity (hypoesthesia or corneal anesthesia) is a characteristic finding of herpetic keratitis, due to the neurotropia of HSV-1.
  • It is evaluated with a cotton swab or the Cochet-Bonnet esthesiometer; asymmetric reduction suggests herpetic versus bacterial or traumatic infection.
  • Loss of sensation may persist after resolution, predisposing to neurotrophic keratopathy and persistent ulcers.
  • In VZV keratitis, hypoesthesia is usually more severe and extensive.

items.title: Topical antivirals
items.contentHtml:
First line

  • The topical treatment: acyclovir or ganciclovir constitutes the first line for herpetic epithelial keratitis.
  • Acyclovir 3% ophthalmic ointment is applied 5 times a day for 7-14 days; ganciclovir 0.15% ophthalmic gel has similar efficacy and better stromal penetration.
  • In children or pregnant women, ganciclovir is preferred due to less systemic toxicity.
  • Topical corticosteroids should not be used in active epithelial keratitis (risk of viral spread and geographic ulcer formation).

items.foco: Avoid topical corticosteroids in epithelial keratitis
items.title: Contraindication of corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Key rule

  • Avoid topical corticosteroids in epithelial keratitis because they suppress the local immune response, allowing uncontrolled viral replication.
  • Their use can convert a simple dendrite into a large geographic ulcer, prolong the disease and increase the risk of corneal scar.
  • Corticosteroids are only indicated in stromal or disciform keratitis (immunological component), always under prophylactic antiviral coverage.
  • In case of diagnostic doubt, never start corticosteroids until active herpetic infection is ruled out.

items.title: Stromal keratitis
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism

  • Stromal keratitis: immune response mediated by T lymphocytes against viral antigens in the stroma, without significant active viral replication.
  • It presents as stromal infiltrates, edema and neovascularization; It can be necrotizing or non-necrotizing.
  • Treatment includes topical corticosteroids (prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone) to control inflammation, along with oral antivirals (acyclovir 400 mg 5 times/day) to prevent epithelial reactivation.
  • Stromal keratitis is the main cause of corneal opacity and visual loss in recurrent herpetic infection.

items.title: Disciform keratitis
items.contentHtml:
Presentation

  • Disciform keratitis: stromal edema is a form of immune-mediated stromal keratitis characterized by a circular or oval area of ​​central stromal edema, with folds in Descemet's membrane.
  • There is no epithelial defect or dense infiltrate; the endothelium shows fine keratic precipitates (KP) and endothelial edema.
  • It is believed to be a delayed hypersensitivity reaction to viral antigens in the deep stroma.
  • Responds to topical corticosteroids (dexamethasone 0.1% every 6 hours) plus prophylactic oral antivirals; resolution may take weeks.

items.foco: Use of oral antivirals in severe cases
items.title: Oral antivirals
items.contentHtml:
Indications

  • The use of oral antivirals in severe cases is indicated in stromal keratitis, disciform keratitis, endothelitis, herpetic uveitis, frequent recurrences or immunosuppression.
  • Acyclovir oral 400 mg 5 times a day for 7-14 days; alternative: valacyclovir 500 mg 2-3 times a day or famciclovir 250 mg 3 times a day.
  • For recurrence prophylaxis (patients with >2 episodes/year), oral acyclovir 400 mg 2 times a day reduces the recurrence rate by ~50%.
  • Oral antivirals do not replace topical treatment in epithelial keratitis, but they are added in extensive cases or with stromal involvement.

items.foco: Frequent recurrence due to latency in the trigeminal ganglion
items.title: Recurrence and latency
items.contentHtml:
Pathophysiology

  • The frequent recurrence due to latency in the trigeminal ganglion explains the chronic and recurrent nature of herpetic keratitis.
  • HSV-1 remains in a latent state in the neurons of the trigeminal ganglion; Factors such as stress, fever, ultraviolet light or immunosuppression can reactivate it.
  • The virus travels through the axon to the cornea, causing new epithelial or stromal infection.
  • The recurrence rate without prophylaxis is 20-50% per year; Oral antiviral prophylaxis significantly reduces relapses.

items.foco: Complication: corneal scar and visual loss
items.title: Scar and visual loss
items.contentHtml:
Complications

  • The complication: corneal scar and visual loss results from fibrotic repair after stromal keratitis or deep geographic ulcers.
  • Corneal opacity can be superficial (leukoma) or deep, and neovascularization worsens visual acuity.
  • Other complications: secondary glaucoma, cataract due to corticosteroids, Neurotrophic keratopathy and corneal perforation (rare).
  • The definitive treatment of the visually significant scar is penetrating keratoplasty, with a high risk of recurrence in the graft (prophylactic use of post-transplant oral antivirals).
